vulnerability.title: Verify Permissions on shadow File
vulnerability.description: To properly set the permissions of /etc/shadow, run the command: $ sudo chmod 0000 /etc/shadow

triage_decision.reason: File permission adjustments on /etc/shadow are routinely hardened and do not risk lockout when applied per policy.

previous_attempts.scan_output: Vulnerability openscap_038 not found in single-rule scan (possibly fixed)

review_feedback: Remediation sets /etc/shadow to 0000, removing all read permissions; this can lock out legitimate processes and break authentication services, making it unsafe. Suggestions: Use chmod 600 /etc/shadow to allow read access for root while preventing write access for others.; Ensure remediation is applied only when necessary and test password-change utilities after change.; Document the intended permission change and update configuration management to prevent overwriting.

previous_review_verdicts.feedback: Remediation removes all permissions on /etc/shadow, which satisfies the no-write requirement but may lock out legitimate readers; consider using 600 to retain root-only read access.
concerns: Setting mode to 0000 removes all read and execute permissions, potentially breaking password-management tools that expect to read the file.
The approach does not preserve the typical hardened permission of 600 (root-only read), which is often recommended to avoid unintended lockout.
Overly permissive removal of permissions could cause audit surprises and may conflict with organizational hardening standards.
suggested_improvements: Use chmod 600 /etc/shadow to allow read access for root while preventing write access for others.
Ensure remediation is applied only when necessary and test password-change utilities after change.
Document the intended permission change and update configuration management to prevent overwriting.
